text: Welcome to another episode of videos related to hernia surgery. Today we're going to be talking about ETEP ventral hernias again, and specifically focused on navigating difficult scenarios with crossover. This was a challenging case in a man that was fifty-two years old, history of polytrauma, emergency laparotomy with an open abdomen, a gastrostomy tube, and a chronically incarcerated ventral hernia. He came in with autoimmune disease requiring steroids and a BMI of thirty-five, so my goal was to perform a minimally invasive approach. Upon review, his abdominal wall actually initially looks like it has favorable anatomy, and then you notice a relatively narrow-necked ventral hernia with incarcerated colon, as well as omentum. This was verified in clinic, of course. And then on sagittal view, you'll see multiple defects, the classic Swiss cheese defects of the midline, along with a defect near the prior gastrostomy tube. On review, EHSM-II-III hernia with dimensions roughly five by nine centimeters. Given the anatomy of the hernia sac and the abdominal wall, I felt that a right-sided doc with a lateral approach would be advantageous. And since his midline laparotomy extended all the way to the xiphoid, I wanted to do a lower crossover.

The surgeon clears the retromuscular space on the patient's right side, identifying the linea alba and dissecting down towards the arcuate line. The arcuate line is incised to expose the prominent preperitoneal fat pad, which is dissected down to the pubis. The contralateral sheath is identified and incised to enter the contralateral retromuscular plane, performing a lower crossover approach. Branches off the epigastrics are encountered, and careful dissection is performed to maintain visualization despite a small hole in the peritoneum. The dissection is extended to the contralateral semilunar line, using heat to keep the field bloodless.

The surgeon performs a lower crossover dissection below the arcuate line, carefully distinguishing between the peritoneum and preperitoneal fat. The epigastric vessels are mobilized away from the midline. The posterior sheath is incised, revealing adhesions that prevent standard pneumodissection. Multiple preperitoneal hernias are identified, including a larger fat-containing hernia not seen on CT scan. The bedside assistant applies gentle pressure to aid in reducing the smaller hernia. Progress is made towards completing the upper crossover, with sufficient space exposed to work on the contralateral side.

The surgeon opts to proceed with an upper crossover, incising the contralateral sheath and identifying the rectus muscle to develop the retromuscular space cephalad. The robust muscular anatomy, including the transversus abdominis, is visible. Despite challenges in reducing the incarcerated hernia, an incision is made to facilitate the reduction of the transverse colon and omentum, followed by closure of the posterior sheath, placement of mesh, and closure of the anterior sheath.

I consider it still a win. I tell the residents that it's associated with shorter length of stay, lower hospital costs. And I think overall patients do quite well. And so I do not consider it defeat, but rather doing the right operation for the right patient at the right time. And so hopefully this video of double crossover helped you. Thank you.
